mappings.rationaleAI: The Crusades covering all major crusades, Crusader states, military orders, and financing directly address the crusading era (A.1), the First Crusade and Latin states (A.1.a), Second and Third Crusades (A.1.b), and later crusades (A.1.d).
The Byzantine Empire from 1081 to 1204 and its interaction with the Crusaders directly address the Byzantine Empire during the crusading era (A.1.c). The Palaeologus restoration connects to A.3.
The Ottoman Empire's rise, conquest of Constantinople (1453), and expansion directly address Turkish Anatolia and the rise of the Ottoman Empire (B.3.b).
The Mongol invasions of eastern Europe and Southwest Asia in the 13th century directly address B.2. The Mongol successor states including the Il-Khans and the Golden Horde relate to B.3.a.
Genghis Khan's unification of Mongol tribes and campaigns into Central Asia and Kievan Rus directly address the Mongolian invasions (B.2).
Timur's defeat of the Golden Horde, Ottoman Empire, and other states directly addresses the Timurids as Il-Khan successor states (B.3.a.i) and Timur's invasion of the Golden Horde (B.3.a.ii).
Turkey's Seljuk and Ottoman history directly addresses the Great Seljuq Empire (B.1) and Turkish Anatolia and the rise of the Ottoman Empire (B.3.b).
Egypt's Fatimid and Mamluk periods directly address the decline of the Fatimids (B.4.a) and the Ayyubids and Mamluks in Egypt and Syria (B.4.b).
Iran's Seljuq dynasty and subsequent Mongol-era Il-Khanate connect to the Great Seljuq Empire in Iran (B.1.a) and the Il-Khans in Iraq and Iran (B.3.a.i).
Spain's Muslim Al-Andalus and the Reconquista connect to the Berber Almoravid and Almohad empires in northwest Africa and Spain (B.4.c).
Cairo founded by the Fatimid dynasty and expanded under Ayyubid and Mamluk rule directly relates to the Ayyubids and Mamluks in Egypt and Syria (B.4.b).
Russia's Kievan Rus and Tatar rule directly address Russia from 1054-1300 with Mongol invasion (A.1.e) and the rise of the Muscovite state under the Golden Horde (A.2.a).
Algeria's Islamic dynasties connect to the Almoravid and Almohad empires (B.4.c) and late medieval dynasties of North Africa (B.4.d).
The Islamic Golden Age's cultural production connects to the Seljuq patronage of the Iranian cultural revival (B.1) and Almohad patronage of philosophy and the arts (B.4.c.ii).

Rumi's Sufi poetry and establishment of the Mevlevi Order in Anatolia after fleeing the Mongol advance connects to religious and intellectual life, specifically Sufism in North Africa and the Islamic world (B.4.d.i).
Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah analyzing cyclical dynastic rise-and-fall in North Africa, Andalusia, and Egypt connects to the late medieval dynasties of North Africa (B.4.d).
The Black Death's origins in Central Asia and transmission via Mongol trade networks connect to the Mongol successor states and their internal disruption (B.3.a).
The Holy Roman Empire's role in papal leadership and participation in the Crusades connects to the background of the First Crusade (A.1.a.i).
Silk Road facilitation by the Mongol Empire connects to trade and administration under the Il-Khans and Mongol successor states (B.3.a).
The Middle Ages broadly addresses the crusading movement and Eastern Christendom (A) and Islamic states (B).
The post-classical period's inter-civilizational trade, crusades, and Mongol Empire broadly address both the crusading movement (A) and the Islamic states (B).
